user_query:
I'm preparing an external audit readiness check on a small demo stack. Please perform a focused security and risk triage of the provided app code and infrastructure snippets and produce two deliverables: (1) a machine-readable findings report at output/findings/report.json and (2) an executive summary at output/findings/summary.md.

Scope and inputs to inspect:
- Application code: input/app/src/app.py
- App configuration: input/app/config/settings.yaml
- Container build: input/app/docker/Dockerfile
- Web server config: input/infra/nginx.conf
- File/permission inventory: input/ops/file_inventory.csv
- Directory contents under input/ (including any secrets such as .env, *.key, *.pem)

Requirements:
1) Inspect the listed code and configuration files and identify concrete security weaknesses or risky defaults. At minimum, include one finding per area: app_code, app_config, container, web_server, file_permissions, and secrets_in_repo.
2) Inspect the input/ directory tree to locate secret-bearing files (.env, *.key, *.pem). List them in the report under a dedicated section, but do not include secret contents—only file paths and a best-effort type (env|key|pem). If you include any excerpts as evidence, redact sensitive values.
3) Use input/ops/file_inventory.csv to evaluate file permission risks. Flag entries that are sensitive by path (.env, settings.yaml, *.key) and/or have permissive modes (e.g., group/world writable or readable beyond principle of least privilege). Include each flagged row with a risk classification and a remediation note.
4) Save output/findings/report.json with this structure:
{
  "generated_at": "ISO-8601 timestamp",
  "scope": ["input/app/src/app.py", "input/app/config/settings.yaml", "input/app/docker/Dockerfile", "input/infra/nginx.conf", "input/ops/file_inventory.csv", "input/** (directory scan)"] ,
  "totals": {"High": n, "Medium": n, "Low": n},
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "string",
      "area": "app_code|app_config|container|web_server|file_permissions|secrets_in_repo",
      "title": "short title",
      "severity": "High|Medium|Low",
      "evidence": {
        "file_path": "relative path",
        "lines_or_keys": ["line numbers or setting keys"],
        "excerpt": "brief non-sensitive snippet if helpful"
      },
      "rationale": "why this is a risk",
      "remediation": "clear, actionable change"
    }
  ],
  "directory_secrets": [
    {"path": "relative path", "type": "env|key|pem"}
  ],
  "permissions_analysis": [
    {"path": "string", "mode": "string", "owner": "string", "risk": "High|Medium|Low", "reason": "why this mode is risky for this file", "recommendation": "stricter mode/ownership change"}
  ]
}
Ensure the report references concrete evidence from the provided files (paths and line numbers or setting names) and does not leak full secret material.
5) Save output/findings/summary.md with a concise executive summary including: Overview (scope and methodology), Risk Posture (counts by severity), Top 3 Risks (one-line each with source file), and Next Steps (prioritized remediation actions). Keep it under ~300 words.

Constraints:
- Work entirely from the provided files and directory structure; do not run external scanners or fetch external resources.
- Do not modify anything under input/.
- All paths in outputs must be relative to the workspace. Redact any secret values to avoid disclosure.

Acceptance: The JSON report should contain at least six findings covering each area listed, with accurate evidence tied to the provided files, a non-empty directory_secrets list if any matches are found under input/, and at least one flagged entry derived from input/ops/file_inventory.csv. The summary must align with the JSON report's counts and top risks. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
